records.ety: [OE. whirlen, probably from the Scand.; cf. Icel. & Sw. hvirfla, Dan. hvirvle; akin to D. wervelen, G. wirbeln, freq. of the verb seen in Icel. hverfa to turn. √16. See Wharf, and cf. Warble, Whorl.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Whirled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Whirling.]

defs: To turn round rapidly; to cause to rotate with velocity; to make to revolve.
quotes: He whirls his sword around without delay.

defs: To remove or carry quickly with, or as with, a revolving motion; to snatch; to harry.
quotes: See, see the chariot, and those rushing wheels, That whirled the prophet up at Chebar flood.
The passionate heart of the poet is whirl'd into folly.

defs: To be turned round rapidly; to move round with velocity; to revolve or rotate with great speed; to gyrate.
quotes: The wooden engine flies and whirls about.
But whirled away to shun his hateful sight.

records.ety: [Cf. Dan. hvirvel, Sw. hvirfvel, Icel. hvirfill the crown of the head, G. wirbel whirl, crown of the head, D. wervel. See Whirl, v. t.]

defs: A turning with rapidity or velocity; rapid rotation or circumvolution; quick gyration; rapid or confusing motion; as, the whirl of a top; the whirl of a wheel.
quotes: The rapid . . . whirl of things here below interrupt not the inviolable rest and calmness of the noble beings above.

defs: Anything that moves with a whirling motion.
quotes: He saw Falmouth under gray, iron skies, and whirls of March dust.

defs: A revolving hook used in twisting, as the hooked spindle of a rope machine, to which the threads to be twisted are attached.
